user_prompt: A user requested deletion of all their data. Generate the DSAR-deletion checklist including which systems to scrub.
actual_output:
**DSAR Deletion Response Checklist — Right to Erasure (Art. 17 GDPR)**

*Disclaimer: This is compliance guidance, not legal advice. All deletion activities must be validated by your internal DPO or qualified legal counsel. Document every step to demonstrate accountability (Art. 5(2)).*

### 1. Intake & Identity Verification (Art. 12(6) & Art. 11)
- [ ] Confirm request was received in writing (email, support ticket, or dedicated privacy portal)
- [ ] Verify identity of the data subject without undue delay (e.g., match email + additional identifier if account exists)
- [ ] Acknowledge receipt within **5 business days** and inform data subject of expected timeline (maximum **30 days**, extendable by 2 months for complex cases with justification)
- [ ] Log request in DSAR register (include date received, requester ID, lawful basis for processing that is now being challenged)

**Critical:** If identity cannot be verified with reasonable certainty, do **not** proceed with deletion. Request additional information.

### 2. Legal Assessment – Check for Erasure Exceptions (Art. 17(3))
- [ ] Determine if any exemption applies:
  - Freedom of expression and information
  - Legal obligation or court order (e.g., tax, anti-money laundering records)
  - Public health or archiving in the public interest
  - Establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims
- [ ] Document which data categories can vs. cannot be deleted
- [ ] If partial deletion applies, clearly explain to the user which data remains and why (Art. 12(3))

**Flag for legal review** any request involving employee data or data processed for fraud prevention.

### 3. Data Flow Mapping – Locate All Instances of the User’s Data
Using the data flow for your SaaS platform (emails, names, usage analytics), search **all** systems using the user’s unique identifier (email + user ID). Typical systems to scrub:

**Primary Systems (Production)**
- [ ] Main application database (PostgreSQL/MySQL) – user table, profile table, subscription table
- [ ] Authentication system (e.g., internal auth, Auth0, Firebase, Cognito) – account records, tokens
- [ ] Application logs (Datadog, ELK, Splunk) – any personally identifiable entries
- [ ] Cloud object storage (AWS S3, Azure Blob) – uploaded documents, profile images, exported reports

**Analytics & Tracking Systems**
- [ ] Analytics platforms (Google Analytics, Mixpanel, Amplitude, Segment, PostHog) – user profiles, event history, behavioral data
- [ ] Advertising platforms (if used) – any audience lists or remarketing data

**Third-Party Processors & Supporting Systems**
- [ ] Email service provider / marketing automation (Mailchimp, SendGrid, Braze, Customer.io)
- [ ] Customer support / chat tools (Intercom, Zendesk, Crisp, Drift)
- [ ] CRM or billing system (Stripe, Chargebee, HubSpot, Salesforce)
- [ ] Data warehouse / BI tools (Snowflake, BigQuery, Looker) – any copied user tables
- [ ] Backup systems (daily/weekly snapshots, long-term archives)
- [ ] Any sub-processors identified in your Article 28 Data Processing Agreements

**Internal / Secondary Systems**
- [ ] Employee email accounts and shared drives (if support tickets or exported CSVs contain user data)
- [ ] Development/staging environments that may contain production data copies
- [ ] Any legacy systems or decommissioned databases still holding archives

**Action:** Update your Records of Processing Activities (Art. 30) with the locations found for this user.

### 4. Execution of Deletion (Art. 17(1) & Art. 28)
- [ ] Delete or irreversibly anonymize data in **all** identified systems
- [ ] For backups: delete where technically feasible; otherwise schedule deletion at next backup cycle and document the retention period
- [ ] Instruct all relevant data processors via formal notice (reference your DPA) to delete the data and obtain written confirmation of completion (Art. 28(3)(g))
- [ ] Remove from any derived datasets (e.g., aggregated analytics where individual is still identifiable)
- [ ] If data was made public (rare for SaaS), take reasonable steps to inform other controllers (Art. 17(2))

**Technical Note:** Use secure deletion methods (e.g., `DELETE` with audit trail, cryptographic erasure for cloud storage). Do not simply set a “deleted” flag if the request is for full erasure.

### 5. Documentation, Notification & Closure (Art. 19 & Art. 12(3))
- [ ] Record all actions taken, systems scrubbed, processors notified, and any exceptions applied
- [ ] Notify the user of completion (or partial completion) within the 30-day window
- [ ] Inform any third parties to whom
judge_reasoning: The chatbot provided a comprehensive and detailed checklist for GDPR data deletion as requested, adhering perfectly to its persona and guidelines.
